title: Machine Vision Lecture 3 Quiz: Image Enhancement and Spatial Filtering
instructions: Answer all 30 multiple-choice questions. Select the best single answer for each item.

questions.prompt: Which best captures the main purpose of image enhancement in machine vision?
options: To improve image quality or interpretability before downstream tasks.
To convert all images to binary format.
To guarantee real-time performance on any hardware.

questions.prompt: What is the key distinction between spatial-domain and frequency-domain enhancement?
options: Spatial methods use color images only, while frequency methods use grayscale only.
Spatial methods operate directly on pixels, while frequency methods operate after a transform.
Spatial methods are nonlinear only, while frequency methods are linear only.
Spatial methods require larger kernels than frequency methods.

questions.prompt: In Lecture 3, the unified spatial enhancement framework is written as:
A point processing operation is characterized by:
options: Using only the current pixel intensity to produce output at that location.
Using a Fourier transform on $8\times 8$ blocks.
Using a neighborhood that is always at least $3\times 3$.
Combining histogram equalization with Sobel filtering.

questions.prompt: For normalized intensity in $[0,1]$, the negative transformation is:
Thresholding is primarily used to:
options: Perform binary segmentation into foreground and background.

questions.prompt: Which family of point transforms has the form $s = c\,\log(1 + r)$?
Why is the $+1$ included in $\log(1+r)$?
options: To make the transform invertible only for integer images.

questions.prompt: The log transform is especially useful when an image has:
options: A wide dynamic range with large high-intensity values dominating display.

questions.prompt: The power-law (gamma) transformation is:
For $\gamma < 1$ in a power-law transform, the typical visual effect is:
For $\gamma > 1$ in a power-law transform, the typical visual effect is:
options: Darkening due to compression of brighter intensities.

questions.prompt: Most consumer displays are modeled with approximately which $\gamma$?
To compensate a display gamma near $2.2$, image pre-correction typically uses gamma near:
Contrast stretching mainly aims to:
options: Remove isolated impulse noise by sorting.
Expand a narrow intensity range to improve visible contrast.
Compute first derivatives in x and y.

questions.prompt: In gray-level slicing, the preserving variant differs from binary slicing because it:
options: Keeps non-target pixels at their original values.
Always sets all non-target pixels to white.
Works only on color channels independently.

questions.prompt: In 8-bit bit-plane slicing, which planes usually carry most coarse visual structure?
options: All planes carry exactly equal visual significance.

questions.prompt: A common reason to hide data in the least significant bit plane is that it:
For discrete levels $r_k$, the histogram is defined as:
The normalized histogram (PMF) is:
If a histogram has two strong peaks separated by a valley, the valley is often used as:
Histogram equalization maps intensities using the:
Local (adaptive) enhancement differs from global equalization because it computes mapping from:
options: A fixed image-wide lookup table only.
Only edge pixels from Sobel output.
Only histogram bins with high counts.

questions.prompt: Which filter is most robust to salt-and-pepper noise while preserving edges?
A key property of smoothing (lowpass) linear filters is that kernel coefficients:
In a typical $3\times 3$ Gaussian approximation, the center coefficient is:
Convolution differs from correlation mainly because convolution:
options: Rotates the kernel by 180 degrees before sliding.
Cannot be implemented in spatial domain.

questions.prompt: When do correlation and convolution produce identical results?
options: When the filter kernel is symmetric under 180-degree rotation.
When image size is a power of two.
Only when zero padding is used.

questions.prompt: In Lecture 3, the Laplacian is described as a ______ derivative operator and is sensitive to ______.
For Sobel edge detection, gradient magnitude is typically computed from $G_x$ and $G_y$ as:
